Ok. Some informations missing to make a big picture
  * how many metrics in ? (from collectors) interval ? (default seems to be 300s)
  * is handler (and his backend) handling batch_size efficiently ?

Anyway I think you can increase batch_size safely. From my experience, value between (metrics nb / collection interval / 2) and (metrics nb / collection interval) are ok.

To explain a bit. Diamond pop "collectors" processes whenever "interval" is reached, construct "metrics" objects and send it into a multiprocessing.Queue (FIFO). Max size of this queue is parameter "metric_queue_size".

In parallel, one single process (handling all handlers sequentially - 2 in your case), consume queue (with process method). Process method trigger sending data (also sequentially - not losing data) to backend every 'batch_size" metrics received. 

This message pops when queue is full meaning handlers (process + send) are too slow to handle incoming data - or - you sent something like "metric_queue_size" at same time.

Hope this helps a bit.

Further investigation pointed that we don't actually need to increase timeout, as the given method already waits for the transaction to be mined.
Instead, we made an approach ( #967 ) of making the first call ([token.deposit](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b490e2ee3fb434780733250f0c5a30879ad3a096/raiden/network/rpc/client.py#L636)) to not wait for the transaction to be mined, then call deposit right after it, and wait only for the later.
This way, [python.py' deposit](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b490e2ee3fb434780733250f0c5a30879ad3a096/raiden/api/python.py#L243) doesn't need to wait too long, as the whole wait is on the `netting_channel.deposit`, which actually waits up to `DEFAULT_POLL_TIMEOUT = 180s` for the block to be mined, but just preempt (usually, only once) through `gevent.sleep` to give opportunity of the events to be processed.
It can be closed as soon as #967 is merged.

I think you might have encountered a proper race-condition for pythreejs. I might have been making too strong assumptions about the order in which certain code paths were taken. I need to look around a little more to make sure.

Technical break down for posterity (all for JS side):
- Widget A is created, it starts creating its three object asynchronously.
- Widget B is created, it starts creating its three object asynchronously.
- A reference to B is added in A. This deserializes the reference to the B widget. This then gets passed through `convertThreeTypeModelToThree()`, in which the `obj` attribute of `B` is attempted read.

The problem is that the syncing code (`convertThreeTypeModelToThree()`) is synchronous, and therefore doesn't check / use the `initPromise` of `B`. I think a lot of code depends on this currently (i.e. syncing being synchronous), so any refactoring will need to be very careful about promises being chained correctly.

@matanster that makes sense, in my experience just consider that if you run inference from the compiled `fasttext` C++ you get like 10-12msec average response time. That said if you use `node.js` (like mine) or `python` wrappers, you have 2 options:

- a native binding header
- a executable fork (e.g. for `node.js` using the `child_process`, etc.) for each model to run as an api
- a queue in front of that executable model in order to handle incoming requests and serve the inference with some policy of your choice.

Regarding `node.js`, if you have a express environment or a node `http` server, it's the best choice in my opinion. You can have a look at some simple benchmarks I did for the simple model provided by facebook as language identification api here:
https://github.com/loretoparisi/fasttext.js/blob/master/benchmarks.md

I agree that it cannot be used as-it-is if you want to achieve the best performances as a web service and if you will to handle concurrency and multi-threading, there is need for a new C++ wrapper for that, but I beat that this is not the aim of this library as it is here, maybe some different repo.

P@k is percentage of labels in top k that are correct. R@k is percentage of
correct labels that are returned in the top k.

Fasttext can indeed learn multiple labels -- at each pass it randomly
samples one of the labels, so after multiple epochs a given X is "pushed"
towards each label an equal number of times.

> *precision* gets incremented by 1.0 when the correct label is seen in the
> list of K labels returned by predict(). *nexamples* is incremented by one
> for each test case. *nlabels* gets incremented by the number of labels in
> the result set which is usually just K.

> In my case I had text with 180 potential labels. Each example gets one and

My hunch is that this is a limitation of the current thread-based system, which maximizes compatibility across all platforms (Android and Windows in particular), in exchange for being slow, due to Python's GIL.

@ot3 I'm not entirely sure the bridge was ignoring the last two of your requests. I would like to know what the initial state of your light is before you executed these commands.

The bridge will set brightness as low as possible when `transitiontime` is provided and the state is currently off and the new state is on. So, when you specify `{'on': True, 'bri': 20, 'transitiontime': 50}` you are telling the bridge to do three things:

1. Turn the light on, if the light is already--noop. If the light is off, turn it on leaving last known state.
2. Set the brightness to 20
3. Execute task over 5 seconds (default of 40ms)
  1. Because we are transitioning from Off -> On, adjust initial brightness to 1.

Your bridge is now trying to adjust the brightness from 1 to 20 over 5 seconds, a very subtle effect.

The second command comes in while the first command is still executing. The bridge has two choices to make, ignore the request or abandon the previous request. But, you are telling the bridge to adjust from whatever the current brightness is (somewhere between 1 and 20) to 200 over a duration of 10 seconds. The bridge abandon's the previous request and starts to increase the brightness to 200. The speed depends on the initial brightness, but it will still be very slow.

The third command now is telling the bridge to turn off the light and take 20 seconds to do it. The bridge abandons the previous request in progress and transitions the brightness from wherever it is between 1 and 200 back to 1, then off.

You may want to try a more obvious approach to testing the `transitiontime`, your commands as listed may be too subtle to notice.

You may also want to enable [`logging`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html) and make sure that phue is actually posting your request to the bridge.

This plot show the size of the effective PSF (PSF convolved with the pixel response) for different WFIRST bandpasses along the horizontal axis. The vertical axis is not important; it it just some naive numerical factor that scales with the effective wavelength . The effective PSFs are drawn on a large ( 64*8, 64*8 ) image with 1/8 of the WFIRST pixel scale.

The PSFs with aberrations are by and large bigger than than those without aberrations. The aberrations are larger for blue bands than for red bands.

At SLAC today, we (@cwwalter, @aaronroodman, @arasmssn) had a bit of brainstorming about the API for this feature with the aim to making it easy to add multiple different models for the sensor.  @craiglagegit already has two models I guess (the full fidelity slow version and a faster one that makes some approximations), and we'd like to get Andy's version in here as well, so users can swap among the different options to compare.  Presumably these would each be at a different point in speed/accuracy space.

I think the sensor code can be reduced down to taking a list of photons with (lambda, image position, photon direction) and an image on which to place the electrons.  We already have the idea of a Silicon class (new on this branch) and PhotonArray (existing, but only x,y,flux currently). 

I think the API for handling the sensor effects can be reduced to a function that takes a PhotonArray (to which we would want to add lambda, thetax, thetay) and the image and the rng.  This function would then add the resulting electrons to the image using whatever sensor model we want to use.

Right now, on this branch, the function that does this is:
```
double PhotonArray::addTo(ImageView<T>& target, UniformDeviate ud, Silicon* silicon=NULL) const;
```
I propose changing this to be a function in the Silicon class:
```
double Silicon::addPhotons(const PhotonArray& phot_array, ImageView<T> target, UniformDeviate ud) const
```
Then different sensor models will be different classes (that derive from a base Sensor class perhaps) that all implement this function.

I volunteer to do the following work on this branch:
* Rewrite parts of the photon shooting code to call this function at the end of the photon shooting process.  
* Edit the PhotonArray class to add lambda, theta values
* Write code to fill the lambda, theta values appropriately (for now just constant values, but with hooks for doing this more properly)
* Write a SimpleSensor class that does the `addPhotons` step in the current way where things are just added directly with no sensor physics.

Then Craig and Andy can focus on just the step where the photons get converted into electrons and drift down through the sensor.  Ideally implementing both models (slow and precise; faster with approximations).
* Move the existing changes to `PhotonArray::addTo` to the Silicon class as a `addPhotons` function.
* Derive the current Silicon class from a virtual base `Sensor` class that just defines the API for this function.
* Maybe change the name to something more descriptive about this particular silicon model with the intent that there will be several models for the user to choose from.
* Also include your high-fidelity model that would apparently be too slow to run at scale, but which we could use for validation studies.

Sound good?

@mdschneider has been working in #716 on the field-dependent optical PSF aberrations, and I've started looking into a different optical PSF-related issue -- representing the obscuration and struts for LSST.   There are three parts to this:

- Just putting in the right obscuration value (we can start the lsst module and tell it this information, i.e., there will be a parameter called ```galsim.lsst.obscuration```, similar to what we have for WFIRST).

- Including the right pattern of struts - for this I am waiting on some info from @aaronroodman , but it's possible that this can be done using a simple variant on our current model for struts (without having to load a full pupil plane image).

- Incorporating field-dependence, i.e., taking into account the fact that the obscuration disk is only perfectly centered if you're at the center of the FOV.  I was thinking we might ignore this as a higher-order effect, and begin by only including field-dependence of the aberrations.

Just started looking at the timings for this branch again.  I had thought that the time required to generate a PSF from a set of phase screens was dominated by the np.ffts needed to go from the pupil plane to the focal plane.  It turns out this isn't right though.  Here's the top of the profiler dump for creating a 6-layer atmospheric PSF:

```
         7865 function calls (7855 primitive calls) in 49.306 seconds

   Ordered by: internal time

   ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
       30   26.371    0.879   26.660    0.889 base.py:957(drawImage)
        1   11.813   11.813   11.816   11.816 interpolatedimage.py:255(__init__)
        5    4.383    0.877   36.830    7.366 phase_psf.py:1149(_step)
       10    2.991    0.299    2.991    0.299 {numpy.fft.fftpack_lite.cfftf}
        6    1.184    0.197   28.684    4.781 {sum}
```

The `drawImage` call in question above is the one I'm wrapping in LookupTable2D.  To get an idea for how much faster this might be, I compared it to scipy.interp2d.  Here's some output from that comparison:

```
SciPy 2D interpolation
Function quintic         took 0.00162079334259 seconds.
Function linear          took 0.000401020050049 seconds.
Function cubic           took 0.000887107849121 seconds.

GalSim 2D interpolation
Function nearest         took 0.00402569770813 seconds.
Function linear          took 0.00383868217468 seconds.
Function cubic           took 0.00575811862946 seconds.
Function lanczos3        took 0.0177404165268 seconds.
Function lanczos5        took 0.0284873962402 seconds.
Function lanczos7        took 0.038597202301 seconds.
Function quintic         took 0.00670549869537 seconds.

GalSim 2D interpolation with edge wrapping
Function nearest_wrap    took 0.00377109050751 seconds.
Function linear_wrap     took 0.00467338562012 seconds.
Function cubic_wrap      took 0.00605549812317 seconds.
Function lanczos3_wrap   took 0.0173172950745 seconds.
Function lanczos5_wrap   took 0.0296632051468 seconds.
Function lanczos7_wrap   took 0.0376654148102 seconds.
Function quintic_wrap    took 0.00597450733185 seconds.
```

So there's probably an order of magnitude or so improvement possible with smarter 2d interpolation.

I'm also a bit surprised by the time for `interpolated image.__init__`, so I'll look into that too.  I think we might be able to use _force_stepk and _force_maxk here to speed it up.

Creates a GraphViz dot file for the specified app names.  You can pass multiple app names and they will all be combined into a single model.  Output is usually directed to a dot file.

Options:

  --pydot               Use PyDot to generate the image.
  -d, --disable-fields  Do not show the class member fields

  -a, --all-applications
                        Automatically include all applications from
                        INSTALLED_APPS
  -o OUTPUTFILE, --output=OUTPUTFILE
                        Render output file. Type of output dependend on file

  -l LAYOUT, --layout=LAYOUT
                        Layout to be used by GraphViz for visualization.
                        Layouts: circo dot fdp neato nop nop1 nop2 twopi
  -n, --verbose-names   Use verbose_name of models and fields
  -L LANGUAGE, --language=LANGUAGE
                        Specify language used for verbose_name localization
  -x EXCLUDE_COLUMNS, --exclude-columns=EXCLUDE_COLUMNS
                        Exclude specific column(s) from the graph. Can also

  -X EXCLUDE_MODELS, --exclude-models=EXCLUDE_MODELS
                        Exclude specific model(s) from the graph. Can also
                        load exclude list from file.
  -I INCLUDE_MODELS, --include-models=INCLUDE_MODELS
                        Restrict the graph to specified models.
  -e, --inheritance     Include inheritance arrows (default)
  -E, --no-inheritance  Do not include inheritance arrows
  -R, --hide-relations-from-fields
                        Do not show relations as fields in the graph.

                        Do not sort fields
  --version             show program's version number and exit

Hey guys - I've had a look, here's a proposal:

(1) **Implementing Retries** - we can probably do this by combining "[retryable](https://github.com/googleapis/gax-python/blob/master/google/gax/retry.py#L73)" in gax-python with the "[response iterator](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/bigtable/google/cloud/bigtable/table.py#L275)" convention currently used in table#read_rows.

Basically, we can wrap the GRPC stream in a callable class that does the following:
- Keeps track of a set of row keys that still need to be read as state in the class
- When the class is called, pop an element off the stream.
- If that element is a valid row, remove that row from the list of keys to be read, and return it.
- If that element is a GRPC error, propagate the Exception up the call stack.

We then take the GRPC wrapper, and wrap it again in a gax-python retryable.  This way, if an idempotent Exception is propagated up from a call, the call will be retried.

(2) **Testing** - we need some new functionality over the current unit testing infrastructure, since those tests make assertions about the RPCs made by the client, and do not take responses into account.  We'll need to add an integration testing framework that will spin up a mock server and make read_rows calls against it.  

@garye's [test script](https://gist.github.com/garye/e7f4fa9694dd5b04580aa7cdd6adf16f) should allow us to model errors sent be the server and the expectation of retries.  So, we'll need to add

- A mock server.  I have access to a go dummy, but  how do we ensure developers can run tests on all platforms?  e.g. if we publish a Linux binary, Mac developers won't be able to run the unit tests.  Do I need to re-implement the server in Python?
- Python tests that spin up a Client instance connecting the server.  This is tricky because the Client API does not seem to support custom configuration values which we need to allow the client to run without credentials, as well as to provide a host and port that points at the locally-running server.  That is, we need the moral equivalent of [BigtableOptionsFactory](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/cloud-bigtable-client/blob/master/bigtable-hbase-parent/bigtable-hbase/src/main/java/com/google/cloud/bigtable/hbase/BigtableOptionsFactory.java#L58).
I broke up the function into table and row, so this works better now.

This is expected behavior. Essentially, The API does not give us one message at a time - it gives us around 50. With the default executor, you will process about 10 at a time. This means that flow control values below a certain threshold have no effect. 

This generally isn't a huge issue. You can make processing serial by having each callback acquire a shared lock - your subscriber will just have several messages waiting to be processing which is normal.

You could also make it serial by providing your own executor with `max_workers=1`, but the subscriber will still queue up pending messages to be processed.

```python
import concurrent.futures

import google.cloud.pubsub_v1.types
import google.cloud.pubsub_v1.policy.thread

flow_control = google.cloud.pubsub_v1.types.FlowControl(...)
policy = google.cloud.pubsub_v1.policy.thread.Policy(
    subscriber, subscription,
    executor=concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1))

policy.open(callback)
```

It might be useful to describe *why* you need to process messages serially. It's definitely not out of the question for us to consider such a feature.

```python
def pytest_collectstart(collector):
    if collector.fspath and collector.fspath.ext == '.ipynb':
        collector.skip_compare += ('stderr',)
```

I like the solution proposed by @NileGraddis; it's simple, it solves the composition problem (and solves it in a way that makes sense when you are dealing with hundreds/thousands of units), and unlike #141 it does not require any changes to NWB. 

Things to consider:

* There is a possibility that by making this part of the spec freely extendable, it will be difficult to handle by analysis tools. This could be mitigated by wrapping the UnitAssociations table in a nice pynwb read API (ideally, NWB users should not need to parse and interpret the table manually). @NileGraddis do you already have an idea of how the read API should look?
* Given that this composition problem appears in so many places, it would be nice if we can come up with a common solution rather than having it solved a different way each time.

@nicain I don't think your round trip example was writing way more data than was in the original file. I was playing around with trying to convert a brain observatory nwb to 2.0, and noticed that all of the timestamps in the Allen nwbs are unique datasets; they aren't soft or hardlinks despite the data in many of them being identical. What I did notice is that the naive implementation of copy-on-write writes without compression, while the Allen nwbs are all gzip compressed. When I hacked in compression the converted file was actually smaller than the original (it also seems to be missing running speed).
